title: I’m chronically unemployed, so I built an arena to let AI agents daytrade crypto 24/7, purely off raw, realtime financial data. And somehow gpt 5 nano is up
author: orange-cola
selftext:
I’ve been curious whether current AI models have any natural aptitude for trading on realtime, raw financial data, without any elaborate news pipelines or convoluted system prompts. I mean literally just raw livestreamed market numbers and a calculator. 



So I built a crypto daytrading arena. All agents consume a realtime stream of ticker data and candlesticks for **BTC**, **SOL**, and **FARTCOIN**. They have access to a calculator and can view their portfolio and holdings. As data flows in, the agent autonomously decides to enter or exit, whenever they want, no guardrails.



I started with four agents, each with $100k in its account: **gpt 5 nano (low reasoning)**, **minimax m2.5**, **grok 4.1 fast (no reasoning)**, and **gemini 2.5 flash**.



After a little more than 24 hrs of continuous trading, here’s roughly where they stand:

* gpt 5 nano: **+$11,500**
* minimax m2.5: +$4000
* gemini 2.5 flash: +$1900
* grok 4.1 fast: -$100



Here's a view of the arena dashboard I've been using to track agent portfolios:

[agent arena dashboard](https://reddit.com/link/1reww2d/video/352q5gcwtqlg1/player)



I'm honestly impressed with how gpt-5-nano has performed so far, considering it's a relatively cheap model. When I started this I definitely wasn't even expecting it to be in the positives by now. It might just be really good at processing raw financial numbers(idk)? I’m keeping these agents running so we’ll see if these gains stay consistent. Eventually I also want to throw in more expensive models (gpt 5.2, sonnet 4.6) and see how they compete too. 



Also, this project is fully open source so you can check it out and run it yourself! [https://github.com/ryan-yuuu/crypto-daytrading-arena](https://github.com/ryan-yuuu/crypto-daytrading-arena)

comments.author: degenbrain
comments.body: Thank you for making this open source. I am creating Binance connector and see how it goes.

replies.author: orange-cola
replies.body: You're welcome! I think I was considering using binance's API as well before I settled on coinbase. Let me know how it goes or if you need any help with the binance connector

replies.author: degenbrain
replies.body: I will submit PR if it's working. How much do you spend on tokens for a 24-hour period?

replies.author: orange-cola
replies.body: It's pretty low cost so far, mostly because I'm using smaller models. My openai bill in the last 24hrs was probably \~$4

replies.author: ukSurreyGuy
replies.body:
brilliant work my friend!

congrats on the infrastructure setup & results

I look forward to playing with your GitHub crypto agent infrastructure

Q. any reason you didn't go for local AI instance? 

it's only low reasoning requirement right

Q. can you convert remote API to local AI eliminating all API costs?

Q. you use free real time price feeds? 

if not you could integrate to MT5...it's got the feeds & ability to export data real time with abit of simple coding for your agents data pipeline

REQUEST : you should also do a YT video on how you setup this rig...I think alot of people want to do even the most basic agent setup integrated with price feed like you

replies.author: orange-cola
replies.body:
Thanks!

>Q. any reason you didn't go for local AI instance?

I don't have the hardware requirements to run LLMs, much less run them fast.

>Q. can you convert remote API to local AI eliminating all API costs?

That's possible if you have the compute power. You'd have to use open source LLM models like deepseek, glm, minimax, etc.

>Q. you use free real time price feeds?

Yes, it's using coinbase's public APIs to get candlestick and ticker data.

Thanks for the rec, I'll look into MT5!

>REQUEST : you should also do a YT video on how you setup this rig...I think alot of people want to do even the most basic agent setup integrated with price feed like you

Hmm I might do that. The github has a pretty straightforward quickstart in the README but I understand it might not be super accessible for non-technical folks. Because of interest I've received, I'm considering providing a website for non-technical users to set up and run agents in the arena by themselves.

comments.author: Dipluz
comments.body: thats cool, You should considering using CCXT, then its easy to just point and click to whatever exchange you want to use. (must admit I havent looked through your entire code base yet)

replies.author: orange-cola
replies.body: Oh cool I've never heard of CCXT, I'll check it out

replies.author: ukSurreyGuy
replies.body:
what is CXXT exactly?

[I had to Google CXXT it says at bottom](https://www.stockgro.club/blogs/stock-market-101/cross-trade/)

What Does CXXT Mean?

CXXT refers to a crossing trade, where buy and sell orders for the same security are matched privately, often across different exchanges. It’s typically used in cross-market or internalized trading systems to facilitate trades without affecting the public market price.

isn't this a problem if your working with bad actor on other side of trade?

replies.author: Dipluz
replies.body: CCXT is an api-wrapper so when you code up which exchanges you want to use and you use CCXT it dosent matter which exchange it is as long as its supported by CCXT. So the whole connect to exchange logic is written with the CCXT library and once your done you have a fair amount of freedom

I'll probably also add an optional switch to account for trading fees by offsetting bids and asks by the fee amount so their built in to the prices already.

replies.author: orange-cola

However, I've made some efforts at realistic trading, like trades always execute at the opposite end of the market's bid-ask spread. And I'll be adding some offset to ticker pricing to build in trading fees into each trade.

comments.author: FigExisting8108
comments.body:
This is actually a really cool experiment.

Most AI trading discussions revolve around sentiment analysis, news pipelines, or heavy quant features, so stripping everything down to raw price data + a calculator is a surprisingly clean way to test whether models have any native pattern intuition for markets.

A few questions that would make this even more interesting:

1. What timeframe are the candlesticks? (1s / 1m / 5m?)  
2. Are the agents allowed to hold multiple positions or only long/flat?  
3. Are they seeing historical context or just streaming ticks?  
4. Do they have any memory of previous trades or reflections on performance?  
5. Are trades constrained by fees / slippage?

Also worth noting: 24 hours in crypto is still a tiny sample size — especially if one of the coins trended strongly. I’d be really curious to see results after a few weeks across different regimes (chop vs trend vs crash).

Another interesting extension might be:
- run 10 instances of each model
- randomize the starting prompt slightly
- compare distribution of outcomes rather than a single agent

That would tell you whether the profit is signal or luck.

Also curious — have you tried platforms like SimianX AI or similar AI trading products? I’ve been experimenting with a few of them recently for AI-driven trading, and it’s interesting to compare how different systems handle raw market data vs structured strategies.

Either way, building an “AI trading arena” is a great idea. If you open source the framework, I suspect a lot of people here would want to run their own model battles.

replies.author: orange-cola
replies.body:
I'm glad you like the project! Btw before I answer your questions, the project is fully open source: [https://github.com/ryan-yuuu/crypto-trading-arena](https://github.com/ryan-yuuu/crypto-trading-arena) 

1. The candlesticks start at 1min and get progressively larger at set intervals farther back in time.

2. Currently, the agents can only hold long/flat positions in their portfolios.

3. In my setup, the agents are seeing up to 3 hours of historical candlesticks. This is configurable, I chose 3hrs just to save on token costs.

4. No memory of previous trades. Although I'd like to experiment on whether providing that context would improve or deteriorate performance. My initial inclination is that exposing previous trade data could introduce bias into an otherwise technicals-only approach. But that's just a guess and I'm curious to see how that would affect performance.

5. Fees are not built into the arena yet. And to roughly approximate slippage, trades only fill at the opposing end of the bid-ask spread. 

I agree that 24 hour performance is not a very strong signal for long term success. I'm keeping the arena running for now to check out its performance over a longer period. Also, I like the idea of running multiple instances for each model and evaluating model perf. based on distribution--I might do that. 

I've never tried any AI trading products before. This is something I wanted to build as a fun experiment.

Good news! It IS open source, you can set up your own arena right now: [https://github.com/ryan-yuuu/crypto-trading-arena](https://github.com/ryan-yuuu/crypto-trading-arena)
